avalancheActivity.comment: Moist and wet avalanches require caution. The avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular at the base of rock walls, and adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls. The wind slabs are in isolated cases prone to triggering. Avalanches can be released, mostly by large loads.

snowpackStructure.comment: Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on shady slopes. The weather conditions gave rise to moistening of the snowpack, especially at low and intermediate altitudes. In general, the snow cover is gradually thinning and varies depending on altitude and exposure.

avalancheActivity.highlights:
Weak layers in the old snowpack necessitate defensive route selection. Shady slopes where weaknesses exist in the old snowpack are especially precarious. In particular shady places that are protected from the wind above approximately 2800 m:
As the moisture increases moist small and medium sized avalanches are possible.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Weak layers in the old snowpack can be released by large additional loads in particular above approximately 2400 m. This applies in particular in shady places that are protected from the wind. Avalanches can penetrate down to the ground and reach large size in isolated cases.
As a consequence of warming during the day, the likelihood of moist and wet avalanches being released will increase on south facing slopes below approximately 2600 m.
Avalanches can occur in particular in starting zones where no previous releases have taken place.

snowpackStructure.comment:
At elevated altitudes a treacherous avalanche situation will be encountered in some localities. Towards its base, the snowpack is faceted. Avalanches can be released in deeper layers, in particular by large additional loads.
Outgoing longwave radiation during the night will be good. The surface of the snowpack has frozen to form a strong crust and will soften during the day. This applies in particular on sunny slopes below approximately 2600 m.

tendency.highlights: From early morning probably danger level 3 (considerable) will be reached in the regions exposed to heavier precipitation above approximately 1800 m.

avalancheActivity.highlights:
Weak layers in the old snowpack necessitate caution and restraint. In particular shady places that are protected from the wind above approximately 2800 m:
As the moisture increases moist small and medium sized avalanches are possible.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Dry avalanches can be released in deeper layers, in particular by large additional loads. The avalanches can in isolated cases reach large size.
As a consequence of warming during the day, the likelihood of moist and wet avalanches being released will increase in particular on steep slopes below approximately 2600 m.

snowpackStructure.comment:
The upper section of the snowpack is well consolidated and its surface has a crust that is strong in many cases. In particular sunny slopes and below approximately 2800 m:
Precarious weak layers exist in the old snowpack especially on steep northwest, north and northeast facing slopes. Several medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large avalanches are possible.

tendency.highlights: From early morning probably danger level 3 (considerable) will be reached in the regions exposed to heavier precipitation above approximately 1800 m.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Wind slabs and wet snow represent the main danger.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Up to 20 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, fell in the last few days above approximately 1800 m. Little snow fell yesterday.
As a consequence of new snow and a sometimes moderate wind from westerly directions, sometimes avalanche prone wind slabs formed by the early morning in particular adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls as well as at high altitude. In particular in the regions exposed to heavier precipitation and on very steep slopes small and medium-sized avalanches are possible. They can in isolated cases be released, even by a single winter sport participant.
As a consequence of solar radiation small and medium-sized moist and wet avalanches are possible below approximately 2600 m. This applies in particular in case of releases originating from steep sunny starting zones at intermediate altitudes that have retained the snow thus far. Even a small snow slide can sweep people along and give rise to falls.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Over a wide area 15 to 20 cm of snow, and up to 30 cm in some localities, fell on Tuesday above approximately 1600 m.
Regions of the south that are exposed to the foehn wind: 2 to 5 cm of snow, and up to 10 cm in some localities, fell on Thursday above approximately 1800 m. The new snow is poorly bonded with the old snowpack in some places below approximately 2200 m.
Intermediate and high altitudes: The new snow is lying on a crust in particular on east to south to west facing aspects. The moderate wind has transported the new snow.

Over a wide area there is still a very large amount of snow.

tendency.highlights: Increase in avalanche danger as a consequence of the snowfall.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Weak layers in the old snowpack necessitate caution and restraint.
avalancheActivity.comment: As a consequence of warming during the day moist avalanches are possible, but they will be mostly small. The natural activity of small avalanches will only slowly increase. Distinct weak layers in the old snowpack necessitate caution. Weak layers in the lower part of the snowpack can be released even by individual winter sport participants in particular at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack, when entering gullies and bowls for example. This applies in particular on northeast, north and northwest facing slopes above the tree line. The avalanches can be released in deep layers of the snowpack and reach medium size.

snowpackStructure.comment: The snowpack remains generally subject to considerable local variations. The rain gave rise to gradual moistening of the snowpack below approximately 1700 m. Above approximately 2000 m northwest, north and northeast facing slopes: Distinct weak layers in the old snowpack represent the main danger.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Weakly bonded old snow requires caution.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Winter sport participants can release avalanches in isolated cases. These can reach large size in isolated cases. The avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular on very steep west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 2000 m. Caution is to be exercised in particular at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack, when entering gullies and bowls for example.

As a consequence of warming during the day and the solar radiation, the likelihood of slab avalanches being released will increase a little in particular on very steep west and east facing slopes below approximately 2200 m.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Above approximately 2000 m: Avalanche prone weak layers exist in the bottom section of the snowpack on west, north and east facing slopes. Towards its surface, the snowpack consists of faceted crystals, especially on shady slopes. In steep terrain there is a danger of falling on the hard snow surface.

Below approximately 2000 m: The old snowpack will be moist.

tendency.highlights: Weakly bonded old snow requires caution.

avalancheActivity.highlights: The backcountry touring conditions remain generally favourable.
avalancheActivity.comment:
The more recent wind slabs represent the main danger. They are easy to recognise but to be assessed with care and prudence.

Mostly small moist snow slides are possible as the day progresses, in the event of solar radiation in particular on extremely steep sunny slopes.
Weak layers in the old snowpack can be released in isolated cases. These avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular on very steep shady slopes above approximately 2500 m and at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack.

snowpackStructure.comment:
In some regions 2 to 10 cm of snow fell on Tuesday above approximately 2200 m. In some regions 5 to 10 cm of snow fell during the night above approximately 2200 m.
Towards its surface, the snowpack is largely stable and its surface has a melt-freeze crust that is strong in many cases.
Especially steep shady slopes high altitudes and the high Alpine regions: Snow that is very compacted on the surface and stabilised by the stormy winds at the end of February, or composed of faceted crystals and, locally, snow with weak cohesion and surface frost. Weak layers exist in the old snowpack here.
Adjacent to ridgelines and in pass areas as well as at high altitude snow depths vary greatly, depending on the infuence of the wind.

tendency.highlights: Over a wide area new snow: The fresh snow as well as the sometimes large wind slabs that are forming over a wide area represent the main danger.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Weakly bonded old snow requires caution. Moist snow slides during the day.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Winter sport participants can release avalanches in isolated cases. These can reach large size in isolated cases. The avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular on very steep west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 2000 m. Caution is to be exercised in particular at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack, when entering gullies and bowls for example.

As a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation snow slides are to be expected. This applies on extremely steep sunny slopes at elevated altitudes. As a consequence of warming during the day and the solar radiation, the likelihood of slab avalanches being released will increase a little in particular on very steep west and east facing slopes below approximately 2200 m.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Some snow has fallen in the Allgäu Alps and in the Western Lechtal Alps. Little snow has fallen on the Main Alpine Ridge and to the north.

Above approximately 2000 m: Avalanche prone weak layers exist in the bottom section of the snowpack on west, north and east facing slopes. Towards its surface, the snowpack consists of faceted crystals, especially on shady slopes. In steep terrain there is a danger of falling on the hard snow surface.

Below approximately 2000 m: The old snowpack will be moist.

tendency.highlights: Weakly bonded old snow requires caution.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Weakly bonded old snow and wet snow require caution.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Weakly bonded old snow requires caution.
Avalanches can be released in the old snowpack, even by a single winter sport participant. They can in isolated cases release deeper layers of the snowpack and reach quite a large size. Avalanche prone locations are to be found especially above approximately 2000 m. These avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular in northwest to north to northeast facing aspects. Caution is to be exercised in particular on steep shady slopes, as well as at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack, when entering gullies and bowls for example.

In the late morning the likelihood of slab avalanches being released will increase in particular on steep south and southwest facing slopes below the tree line.

As a consequence of warming during the day individual mostly small wet loose snow avalanches are possible.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Shady slopes: Distinct weak layers exist in the bottom section of the snowpack in particular on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes. This applies especially above approximately 2000 m.

Outgoing longwave radiation during the night was quite good over a wide area. The surface of the snowpack will freeze, but a strong crust will not form and will soften during the day. This applies in particular below approximately 2600 m.

Sunny slopes: The solar radiation will give rise as the day progresses to rapid moistening of the snowpack.

tendency.highlights: Over a wide area 5 to 15 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, will fall from the afternoon above approximately 1600 m. The avalanche danger will increase a little during the day.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Wet and gliding avalanches are the main danger.

snowpackStructure.comment: In gullies and bowls above approximately 1800 m a lot of snow is lying. The spring-like weather conditions gave rise to moistening of the snowpack. These conditions brought about a gradual weakening of the snowpack also at intermediate and high altitudes. Some new snow above approximately 1800 m. Backcountry tours, off-piste skiing and ascents to alpine cabins should be started and concluded very early.

avalancheActivity.highlights: The backcountry touring conditions are mostly favourable.
avalancheActivity.comment:
In some localities up to 10 cm of snow, but less in some localities, fell in the last few days above approximately 2000 m. The new snow is lying on top of a quite favourable old snowpack.
As a consequence of solar radiation individual small moist and wet avalanches are possible. This applies in particular on extremely steep sunny slopes, as well as in steep rocky terrain.

In particular in the regions of the north that are exposed to the foehn wind the avalanche prone locations are a little more prevalent and the danger is greater. Even a small snow slide can sweep people along and give rise to falls.

Weak layers in the old snowpack can be released in isolated cases and mostly by large additional loads especially at elevated altitudes. This applies in particular on extremely steep shady slopes above approximately 2700 m.

snowpackStructure.comment:
5 to 15 cm of snow fell on Tuesday above approximately 1600 m. Regions of the north that are exposed to the foehn wind 2 to 5 cm of snow fell on Thursday above approximately 1800 m.
The old snowpack is well consolidated and its surface consists of loosely bonded snow lying on a melt-freeze crust that is strong in many cases.
Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise from the middle of the day to gradual moistening of the snowpack in particular on steep sunny slopes at intermediate altitudes.
Intermediate and high altitudes: Individual weak layers exist in the snowpack especially on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes.
In particular in the vicinity of peaks a little snow is lying.

tendency.highlights: Increase in avalanche danger as a consequence of the snowfall.

avalancheActivity.comment: Above approximately 1400 m small moist and wet avalanches are possible.

avalancheActivity.highlights: The backcountry touring conditions remain generally favourable.
avalancheActivity.comment:
The snowpack remains generally stable.

As a consequence of the new snow small moist snow slides are possible as the day progresses, in the event of solar radiation in particular on very steep sunny slopes.
Weak layers in the old snowpack can be released in very isolated cases. These avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular on extremely steep shady slopes above approximately 2400 m and at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack.

snowpackStructure.comment:
In some regions 2 to 5 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, has fallen above approximately 2200 m.
Towards its surface, the snowpack is largely stable and its surface has a melt-freeze crust that is strong in many cases. In steep terrain there is a danger of falling here.
Especially steep shady slopes high altitudes and the high Alpine regions: Snow that is very compacted on the surface and stabilised by the stormy winds at the end of February, or composed of faceted crystals and, locally, snow with weak cohesion and surface frost. Weak layers exist in the old snowpack here.
Adjacent to ridgelines and in pass areas as well as at high altitude snow depths vary greatly, depending on the infuence of the wind.

tendency.highlights: Fresh snowfall with heavier amounts in the south-east of the region: The fresh snow as well as the sometimes large wind slabs that are forming over a wide area represent the main danger.

regions.name: B - VALLI DI GRESSONEY, AYAS, CHAMPORCHER

avalancheActivity.highlights: Weak layers in the old snowpack can be released in isolated cases. The danger of moist and wet avalanches will increase during the day.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Avalanches can in isolated cases be released in the old snowpack and reach medium size in isolated cases on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes. They can be released, even by a single winter sport participant.
In the late morning the likelihood of slab avalanches being released will increase in particular on steep southeast and southwest facing slopes below approximately 2400 m. As a consequence of warming during the day individual mostly small wet loose snow avalanches are possible.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Outgoing longwave radiation during the night was quite good over a wide area. The surface of the snowpack will freeze, but a strong crust will not form and will soften during the day. This applies in particular below approximately 2600 m.

This applies especially above the tree line. Avalanche prone weak layers exist in the old snowpack in particular on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes.

Sunny slopes: The solar radiation will give rise as the day progresses to rapid moistening of the snowpack.

tendency.highlights: Over a wide area 5 to 15 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, will fall from late morning above approximately 1600 m. The avalanche danger will increase a little during the day.

avalancheActivity.comment: Only isolated dry avalanches are possible. They can be released, in particular by large loads, but they will be small in most cases. Individual avalanche prone locations are to be found on steep shady slopes at high altitude.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Shady slopes: Avalanche prone weak layers exist in the old snowpack in particular on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes.

Sunny slopes: The weather conditions gave rise to moistening of the snowpack.
